reasoning: The Federal Reserve's total liquidity and credit facilities loans currently exhibit minor weekly fluctuations around a generic baseline of ~$4.5-$4.8 billion, representing a 'calm' period without major stresses. In environments without systemic banking stress, typical discount window usage resembles spikes (when banks need short-term funding) followed by rapid decay as the short-term loans mature. This creates an asymmetric distribution of weekly changes: a few larger week-over-week increases and more frequent, smaller week-over-week decreases (e.g., of the last six weeks, 4 were down and 2 were up). Recent data shows a sideways-to-slightly-downward trend, ending at 4714.0. Without impending stress or a specific facility opening, the chance of a week-over-week increase is slightly less than 50% due to the structural tendency of routine loan roll-offs. Combined with the statistical forecast prior of 0.46, a 46% probability is the most reasonable estimate.
